title: How Fruit Flies Manage Their Exceptionally Long Sperm
gist: A Nature Physics study using high-speed microscopy at the Flatiron Institute finds fruit-fly sperm — nearly as long as the whole fly — avoid tangling inside a tiny seminal vesicle by moving in coordinated wave-like flows.

insights.claim: Fruit-fly sperm, each about eight-tenths of an inch (nearly the fly's body length), pack by the thousands into a seminal vesicle just eight-hundredths of an inch wide yet avoid tangling by moving in wave-like flows back and forth across the vesicle.
insights.speaker: Devin Reese
insights.so_what: A model for self-organizing active matter; basic-science curiosity with limited direct market read-through.
insights.quote: the researchers saw the sperm moving in wave-like flows back and forth across the vesicle
insights.locator: Instead, the researchers saw the sperm

insights.claim: Each sperm tows its long tail rather than entangling, which lead author Jasmin Imran Alsous of the Flatiron Institute's Center for Computational Biology called what 'really blew my mind.'
insights.speaker: Devin Reese
insights.so_what: Computational-biology imaging continues to reveal emergent order in crowded biological systems.
insights.quote: they’re not getting entangled. That’s what really blew my mind.
insights.locator: Each sperm towed its long tail
